headline: Finalized Lido withdrawals remain claimable on-chain, but request placement and finalization can be paused and a governance-linked path can prolong that pause.

findings.text: The user-facing Ethereum withdrawal exit functions on WithdrawalQueueERC721 are `requestWithdrawals`, `requestWithdrawalsWstETH`, `requestWithdrawalsWithPermit`, `requestWithdrawalsWstETHWithPermit`, `claimWithdrawals`, and `claimWithdrawal`; the deployed mainnet withdrawal queue proxy is `0x889edC2eDab5f40e902b864aD4d7AdE8E412F9B1` and its implementation is `0xE42C659Dc09109566720EA8b2De186c2Be7D94D9`.

Request-placement functions call `_checkResumed()` and therefore revert while the queue is paused, whereas `claimWithdrawals` and `claimWithdrawal` have no pause guard and instead rely on ownership/finalization checks: they revert unless the caller owns the request and the request is finalized and unclaimed.

`pauseFor(uint256)` and `pauseUntil(uint256)` are both restricted to `PAUSE_ROLE`, and the contract explicitly supports `PAUSE_INFINITELY`; current documented holders of the withdrawal queue `PAUSE_ROLE` are GateSeal Withdrawal Queue (`0x8A854C4E750CDf24f138f34A9061b2f556066912`) and the Reseal Manager (`0x7914b5a1539b97Bd0bbd155757F25FD79A522d24`).

Lido distinguishes a fast emergency pause from governance-linked control: the GateSeal committee can trigger a one-time emergency seal on WithdrawalQueue for a fixed 14-day window, while the Reseal Manager can extend an existing temporary pause into a governance-controlled pause tied to non-Normal Dual Governance phases; upgrades and proxy admin control sit with the Lido DAO Agent and require DAO voting.

The withdrawal queue is FIFO, request placement is effectively unbounded in count, and each individual request is bounded between `MIN_STETH_WITHDRAWAL_AMOUNT = 100` wei and `MAX_STETH_WITHDRAWAL_AMOUNT = 1000 ether` worth of stETH per request; request placement and finalization are the pausable parts of the queue, but I did not find a hard documented maximum wait time or a simple documented daily withdrawal cap in the checked allowed sources.

I found no permissionless forced-exit or escape-hatch function for users in the WithdrawalQueue contract; the checked contract source exposes ordinary request/claim flows plus oracle/finalization and pause controls, but no separate adversarial-admin bypass for queued withdrawals.

The deployed implementation is verified on Etherscan and exposes both `Read Contract` and `Write Contract` tabs, so the exit functions are directly callable on-chain without relying on Lido’s frontend.

steelman.red: Because the contract supports `PAUSE_INFINITELY` and current `PAUSE_ROLE` is not limited to a pure DAO vote path, a governance-linked pause extension could leave new withdrawal requests and finalization blocked for an unbounded period.
steelman.orange: The strongest fit is orange because already-finalized withdrawals remain claimable, but request placement and finalization can still be paused, first by a fast 14-day GateSeal emergency path and then potentially prolonged through the Reseal Manager into governance-controlled pause state.
steelman.green: The best green argument is that the actual user claim path for finalized withdrawals is on-chain and not pause-gated, and the fast emergency multisig path itself is capped at 14 days rather than being an uncapped unilateral multisig freeze.

rationale.verdict: Choosing orange because the most important user-protective fact is that `claimWithdrawals` / `claimWithdrawal` stay callable for already-finalized exits, but Lido still retains a documented path to pause request placement and finalization, with an emergency GateSeal capped at 14 days and a separate governance-linked reseal path that can prolong the pause beyond that window.

evidence.shows: WithdrawalQueue](https://github.com/lidofinance/core/blob/master/contracts/0.8.9/WithdrawalQueue.sol%22,%22shows%22:%22WithdrawalQueue) source code defining `PAUSE_ROLE`, `RESUME_ROLE`, `pauseFor`, `pauseUntil`, `resume`, request functions, claim functions, and the fact that claims of finalized requests remain available while placement/finalization are paused.

Mainnet](https://github.com/lidofinance/core/blob/master/deployed-mainnet.json%22,%22shows%22:%22Mainnet) deployment registry mapping WithdrawalQueueERC721 proxy `0x889edC2eDab5f40e902b864aD4d7AdE8E412F9B1` to implementation `0xE42C659Dc09109566720EA8b2De186c2Be7D94D9`, and listing GateSeal / committee metadata in the same deployment manifest.

Deployed](https://etherscan.io/address/0x889edc2edab5f40e902b864ad4d7ade8e412f9b1%22,%22shows%22:%22Deployed) Lido Withdrawal Queue proxy on Ethereum mainnet, confirming the live contract address users interact with for withdrawal requests/claims.

Verified](https://etherscan.io/address/0xe42c659dc09109566720ea8b2de186c2be7d94d9?age=90D&amt=0-1%22,%22shows%22:%22Verified) WithdrawalQueueERC721 implementation on Etherscan with `Read Contract` and `Write Contract` tabs exposed, supporting direct on-chain interaction without Lido’s frontend.

Lido](https://github.com/lidofinance/docs/blob/main/docs/guides/protocol-levers.md%22,%22shows%22:%22Lido) governance-levers documentation listing current holders for WithdrawalQueueERC721 `PAUSE_ROLE` and `RESUME_ROLE`, stating that the GateSeal mechanism is time-limited and that the Reseal Manager can extend a seal window while upgrades require a DAO vote through the Aragon Agent.

GateSeal](https://github.com/lidofinance/docs/blob/main/docs/contracts/gate-seal.md%22,%22shows%22:%22GateSeal) documentation stating that the WithdrawalQueue GateSeal is a one-time panic button, the sealing committee is a multisig-like committee, and the seal duration is limited to 14 days; it also explains the ResealManager governance-linked extension path.

Deployed](https://etherscan.io/address/0x8A854C4E750CDf24f138f34A9061b2f556066912%22,%22shows%22:%22Deployed) GateSeal contract for WithdrawalQueue, with constructor/view surface showing sealing committee, seal duration, sealables, and expiry for the live emergency-pause contract.

unknowns: E5: I did not find a hard documented maximum withdrawal-queue duration or a simple documented daily withdrawal cap for users in the checked allowed sources; the sources I checked document FIFO ordering, per-request size bounds, and waiting-time estimation tooling, but not a guaranteed maximum wait.

E6: I did not find a dedicated permissionless escape hatch beyond the standard request-and-claim flow, but proving absence exhaustively from the allowed sources is hard; I found no function explicitly labeled as emergency exit / forced user exit / escape hatch in the live queue source I checked.
